content.text: Identity and Access Management (IAM) for AI agents
is the set of systems and practices that establish
what the agent is allowed to do

— software processes that act on behalf of users or autonomously — and they require the same identity lifecycle management as human employees: provisioning, credential issuance, policy enforcement, access review, and revocation.
(IAM Roles for Service Accounts, for AWS integration).

Traditional human-user IAM assumes that a credential (password, API key) is owned and controlled by a specific person. AI agents violate this assumption: they run at scale, are ephemeral, and can be cloned or replicated. Without strong workload identity:
You cannot answer "which specific agent instance made this API call" — only "some pod in the agents namespace".
Revoking a compromised agent's access requires finding and deleting all static credentials it holds — a race condition against an attacker.
You cannot enforce "agent X may only use tool Y if the requesting user has role Z" without a policy engine that understands agent identity alongside user context.
SPIFFE assigns each agent pod a cryptographic identity (an X.509 SVID with a SPIFFE URI like
) that is automatically rotated, tied to the specific workload (not the node), and verifiable without a central credential database.
SPIFFE SVIDs are X.509 certificates issued to specific pods based on attestation of their Kubernetes node and pod identity. They cannot be forged without access to the SPIRE CA.
SPIRE rotates SVIDs before they expire (default 1 hour TTL) without any manual intervention. The SPIRE Agent on each node serves the new SVID to the workload via the Workload API.
Authorization logic is written in Rego and stored in Git. Every decision is auditable, testable with
, and deployable via CI/CD — not embedded in application code.
On AWS EKS, IRSA lets a Kubernetes ServiceAccount assume an IAM role by exchanging its projected OIDC token for short-lived AWS STS credentials — no static AWS access keys needed in the cluster.

OPA can combine agent identity, user context (the human who triggered the agent), time of day, and resource sensitivity in a single policy decision — enabling fine-grained controls impossible with role-based systems alone.

SPIRE requires running a SPIRE Server (HA-recommended), SPIRE Agents on each node, and maintaining registration entries for each workload. Misconfigured attestation rules can prevent pods from getting SVIDs.
Rego has a learning curve. Incorrect policies that accidentally deny legitimate access are hard to debug without structured logging and a local evaluation environment.
If OPA is unavailable when an agent makes an authorization request, the request fails. OPA must be run with redundancy and its bundle sync failures must be alarmed.
Each agent type requires its own IAM role with an OIDC trust policy referencing the specific Kubernetes namespace and ServiceAccount. At scale, IAM role proliferation becomes a management challenge.
The identity and authorization flow for a Kubernetes-hosted agent has three phases:

When a pod starts, the SPIRE Agent on the same node contacts the SPIRE Server and presents proof of the pod's identity (namespace, ServiceAccount name, pod UID) using the Kubernetes node's credentials. The SPIRE Server verifies this proof against the Kubernetes API and, if the pod's identity matches a registered entry, issues an X.509 SVID and private key pair.

For cloud resource access (AWS, GCP), the agent uses its projected Kubernetes ServiceAccount JWT as an OIDC token. An STS call exchanges this JWT for short-lived cloud credentials. The cloud IAM system trusts the Kubernetes OIDC provider because the cluster's OIDC discovery endpoint was registered with the cloud.

Before executing a tool call, the agent (or a sidecar/gateway) sends an authorization query to OPA with the agent's SVID principal, the requested resource (tool name, API endpoint), and any user context. OPA evaluates the Rego policy bundle and returns allow or deny.

attrs.alt: IAM for agents: Agent Pod with K8s ServiceAccount and projected OIDC token flows through SPIRE for SVID issuance, then OPA for policy evaluation, reaching LLM API, Vector DB, Tool Registry, and AWS services

content.text: The agent pod holds a Kubernetes ServiceAccount (its K8s identity) and a projected OIDC JWT (for cloud federation). SPIRE issues an X.509 SVID for cryptographic peer authentication. OPA evaluates Rego policies combining the agent's SVID principal and user context before allowing access to any resource.
Multi-agent architectures where different agent types have different privilege levels — a summarizer agent should have fewer permissions than an orchestrator.
Agents that access cloud services (S3, RDS, Secrets Manager) on AWS EKS — IRSA eliminates all static AWS credentials from the cluster.
Platforms where authorization logic is complex and evolves — OPA policies can be updated without redeploying agents, and policy changes are diff-reviewed in Git.
Regulated environments where auditors require evidence that "only agents with role X accessed resource Y" — OPA's decision log provides this evidence.
Any environment where the service mesh is already using Istio — SPIRE is the standard identity backend for Istio's mTLS and AuthorizationPolicy, so it may already be present.
Setting up IRSA on EKS: associate a Kubernetes ServiceAccount with an IAM role so the agent can call AWS APIs without static credentials.
Agent code using IRSA — the SDK automatically exchanges the projected OIDC token for STS credentials:

import boto3
import os

def get_agent_session() -> boto3.Session:
    """
    On EKS with IRSA, the boto3 SDK automatically reads the projected
    ServiceAccount token from the path in AWS_WEB_IDENTITY_TOKEN_FILE
    and exchanges it for STS credentials via the role in AWS_ROLE_ARN.
    No explicit credential handling needed.
    """
    # These env vars are injected automatically by EKS when IRSA is configured:
    # AWS_WEB_IDENTITY_TOKEN_FILE=/var/run/secrets/eks.amazonaws.com/serviceaccount/token
    # AWS_ROLE_ARN=arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/agent-research-role
    # AWS_ROLE_SESSION_NAME=research-agent
    assert os.environ.get("AWS_WEB_IDENTITY_TOKEN_FILE"), (
        "AWS_WEB_IDENTITY_TOKEN_FILE not set — is IRSA configured for this ServiceAccount?"
    )
    return boto3.Session()  # SDK uses WebIdentityTokenFile automatically


def read_document(bucket: str, key: str) -> str:
    session = get_agent_session()
    s3 = session.client("s3")
    response = s3.get_object(Bucket=bucket, Key=key)
    return response["Body"].read().decode("utf-8")


# OIDC client credentials flow for non-AWS resources
import httpx
import json

def get_oidc_token(token_endpoint: str, client_id: str, client_secret: str) -> str:
    """
    Exchange agent credentials for a short-lived access token via OAuth2
    client_credentials grant. Used for non-AWS APIs that support OIDC.
    """
    response = httpx.post(
        token_endpoint,
        data={
            "grant_type": "client_credentials",
            "client_id": client_id,
            "client_secret": client_secret,
            "scope": "agent:read tool:execute",
        },
        headers={"Content-Type": "application/x-www-form-urlencoded"},
    )
    response.raise_for_status()
    token_data = response.json()
    return token_data["access_token"]  # short-lived (e.g., 1 hour)
OPA Rego policies for fine-grained agent authorization:

A healthcare AI platform operates patient-facing diagnostic agents and internal research agents on EKS. Patient data is subject to HIPAA, and regulatory auditors require evidence that only specific agent types accessed patient records.
The platform implemented a three-layer IAM model:
SPIRE issues unique SVIDs to each agent type. The diagnostic agent has SVID
, the research agent has a separate SVID. No two agent types share an identity.
IRSA grants the diagnostic agent an IAM role with read-only access to the patient records S3 bucket. The research agent's IAM role grants access only to de-identified research datasets.
OPA policies enforce that the diagnostic agent may only call the EHR API if the requesting user (the clinician) has the
role for the specific patient ID in the request.

During a HIPAA audit, the team produced OPA decision logs showing every patient data access: which agent SVID, which user, which patient record, timestamp, and allow/deny decision. This replaced a manual access review process that had previously taken weeks.

Using a single Kubernetes ServiceAccount across all agent types means all agents share the same IRSA role and SPIFFE identity. You cannot enforce per-agent authorization policies or audit per-agent access.
Not configuring OPA bundle sync failure alerting:
If OPA fails to pull a new policy bundle, it continues evaluating with the old bundle. Silent policy staleness means security fixes (adding a deny rule) are not applied until you notice the lag.
condition allows any workload in the cluster to assume the role by presenting any OIDC token. Always scope the trust policy to the specific namespace and ServiceAccount.
Running OPA with no decision logging:
OPA's decision log is the primary audit artifact. If it is not enabled, you cannot produce evidence of which agent accessed which resource — defeating the purpose of using OPA for compliance.
Mixing user context and agent identity in a single token:

Some designs pass the user's JWT directly to the agent to forward to APIs. This conflates user identity with agent identity and prevents per-agent access controls. Keep agent identity (SVID) separate from user context (passed as structured data in the authorization query).
One ServiceAccount per agent type, named after the agent:
) makes IRSA role bindings, SPIRE registrations, and OPA policies self-documenting.
Store OPA policies in Git and deploy via CI/CD:
Policy changes should go through code review. Use
in CI to verify that policy changes don't accidentally deny legitimate access paths before deploying.
) outputs intermediate evaluation state, making Rego debugging much faster.
Deploy SPIRE Server in HA mode:
A SPIRE Server outage prevents new SVIDs from being issued. Agents with expiring SVIDs will fail authentication. Run 2+ SPIRE Server replicas backed by the same SQL datastore.
Scope IRSA roles to specific S3 key prefixes and secret ARNs:
to prevent cross-agent data access within the same bucket.

SPIRE scales to tens of thousands of workloads on a 3-node HA cluster. The SPIRE Agent runs as a DaemonSet and serves SVIDs from a local cache — most SVID fetches are served in-memory without a round trip to the SPIRE Server. SVID rotation is staggered: the Agent renews SVIDs at 50% of their TTL, so a 1-hour SVID is renewed every 30 minutes in the background without blocking the workload.

OPA's policy evaluation latency is typically sub-millisecond for simple policies. For complex Rego policies with large data bundles (e.g., thousands of user-role mappings), partial evaluation and caching reduce per-request overhead. OPA's gRPC interface handles 50,000+ decisions per second on a single instance; run 2+ replicas behind a load balancer to eliminate the single point of failure.
CNCF project for production SPIFFE implementation. Provides a Kubernetes workload attestor that verifies pod identity against the K8s API. Integrates with Istio, Envoy, and Vault.
CNCF graduated project for policy-as-code. Run as a sidecar or standalone service. OPA Gatekeeper integrates OPA with Kubernetes admission control for policy enforcement at deployment time.
Commercial OPA management plane from the OPA creators. Provides a UI for writing, testing, and deploying Rego policies with built-in impact analysis.
command creates the IAM role, trust policy, and K8s ServiceAccount annotation in a single operation — the recommended way to set up IRSA.
Teleport's Machine ID feature issues short-lived SSH and TLS certificates to agent workloads using workload attestation, providing a simpler alternative to SPIRE for SSH access patterns.
OIDC identity providers for the client_credentials OAuth2 flow when agents need to authenticate to internal APIs that support OIDC. Issue short-lived JWT access tokens scoped to agent identities.
Default SPIRE SVID TTL is 1 hour. A stolen SVID is only valid until its TTL expires — unlike a static API key which remains valid until explicitly revoked.
The SPIRE Server's upstream CA (used to sign SVIDs) should be backed by AWS ACM PCA, GCP Certificate Authority Service, or Vault PKI — not a self-generated CA stored on disk.

Configure your OPA integration to fail-closed (deny on OPA timeout) for security-critical decisions. Fail-open (allow on timeout) is acceptable only for non-critical paths where availability matters more than security.

EKS projected ServiceAccount tokens expire after 24 hours by default (configurable, minimum 10 minutes). The kubelet rotates them automatically — but agents that cache the token path rather than re-reading the file will use an expired token.
: a person's face, fingerprint, or password proves they are who they claim to be. Agent IAM needs the equivalent for software processes — but software cannot show a face.
SPIFFE is the badge system for workloads

: the SPIRE Agent cryptographically proves "this process is the research-agent ServiceAccount running in the agents namespace on this specific Kubernetes node" and issues a short-lived badge (SVID) to prove it.

: it checks the badge (SVID), looks up the door's access list (Rego policy), and considers the context (time of day, user role) before deciding to let the agent through. All decisions are logged in a ledger. When an auditor asks "who accessed patient record 12345?", you read the ledger rather than interviewing witnesses.
Treat AI agents as non-human principals with their own identity lifecycle — one ServiceAccount and SPIFFE SVID per agent type, not shared credentials across all agents.
SPIFFE X.509 SVIDs are cryptographically unforgeable, automatically rotated, and tied to the specific workload — the gold standard for machine identity over static API keys.
IRSA on EKS eliminates all static AWS credentials from Kubernetes: the agent's ServiceAccount JWT is exchanged for short-lived STS credentials automatically by the SDK.
OPA Rego policies encode authorization logic that can combine agent identity, user context, resource sensitivity, and time — expressing rules impossible with simple role-based access control.
Enable OPA decision logging on day one — it is the primary audit artifact for proving "only authorized agents accessed sensitive resources".
Store Rego policies in Git, test them with
in CI, and deploy via CD — policy changes should go through the same review process as application code.
OPA fail-closed behavior (deny on timeout) is the correct default for security-sensitive agent authorization decisions, even at the cost of occasional availability impact.
